sources.notes: Statement and constraints: for each i<n, at most once apply a_i := a_i xor a_{i+1}; sum n <= 200000.
Official code confirms checking a_i, a_i xor a_{i+1}, and a_i xor b_{i+1} for each position.
Chinese write-up explains the three order cases and operation dependencies.
Independent notes confirm each position changes at most once and candidates are {a_i, a_i xor a_{i+1}, a_i xor b_{i+1}}.

annotation.summary: Given arrays a and target b, each position i<n may xor a_i with its right neighbor at most once; decide whether a can become b.
annotation.constraints: Multiple tests, sum n <= 200000, values below 2^30; O(n) check required.
annotation.core_idea: a_n never changes, so it must equal b_n. For i<n, the final a_i has only three possible sources: no operation gives a_i; operating before i+1 changes gives a_i xor original a_{i+1}; operating after i+1 has reached b_{i+1} gives a_i xor b_{i+1}. If every b_i is among these candidates, the dependencies can be ordered acyclically, so the transformation is feasible.
tricks: Separate operation before/after the right neighbor changes.

solution_variants.name: three-candidate-local-check
solution_variants.summary: Check a_n=b_n, then for each i<n test whether b_i lies in {a_i, a_i xor a_{i+1}, a_i xor b_{i+1}}.

tags.evidence: a_n is immutable, and once the right target is fixed each position has a small invariant-derived reachable set.
Each position is fully characterized by three mutually exclusive cases: no operation, operation before the right neighbor changes, or operation after it changes.
The operation is XOR, but there is no high-dimensional bitmask state or bitwise optimization.
